results.title: Estructuras heterárquicas en la red antimonopolio en Brasil
results.summary: This article examines the heterarchical structures shaping Brazil’s anti-monopoly network, focusing on interactions among regulatory agencies, technocratic elites, international organizations, and private actors. Drawing on critical political economy and the concept of heterarchy (Cerny), the analysis shows how regulatory authority is distributed across overlapping national and transnational nodes, producing fragmented forms of state power. Based on documentary analysis and secondary sources, the study demonstrates that Brazil’s competition regime combines formal institutional autonomy with deep embeddedness in global governance networks, particularly those linked to the oecd and international competition forums. This configuration promotes regulatory convergence while constraining democratic accountability and the state’s developmental capacity. The article argues that anti-monopoly institutions operate less as mechanisms to curb economic concentration than as devices stabilizing the neoliberal order, reinforcing elite influence through technocratic expertise and revolving-door dynamics.

results.title: AgentCity: Constitutional Governance for Autonomous Agent Economies via Separation of Power
results.summary: Autonomous AI agents are beginning to operate across organizational boundaries on the open internet -- discovering, transacting with, and delegating to agents owned by other parties without centralized oversight. When agents from different human principals collaborate at scale, the collective becomes opaque: no single human can observe, audit, or govern the emergent behavior. We term this the Logic Monopoly -- the agent society's unchecked monopoly over the entire logic chain from planning through execution to evaluation. We propose the Separation of Power (SoP) model, a constitutional governance architecture deployed on public blockchain that breaks this monopoly through three structural separations: agents legislate operational rules as smart contracts, deterministic software executes within those contracts, and humans adjudicate through a complete ownership chain binding every agent to a responsible principal. In this architecture, smart contracts are the law itself -- the actual legislative output that agents produce and that governs their behavior. We instantiate SoP in AgentCity on an EVM-compatible layer-2 blockchain (L2) with a three-tier contract hierarchy (foundational, meta, and operational). The core thesis is alignment-through-accountability: if each agent is aligned with its human owner through the accountability chain, then the collective converges on behavior aligned with human intent -- without top-down rules. A pre-registered experiment evaluates this thesis in a commons production economy -- where agents share a finite resource pool and collaboratively produce value -- at 50-1,000 agent scale.

results.title: India’s Foreign Policy, Geostrategic Trade, and National Power in an AI-Driven World
results.summary:
Abstract
This study investigates how trade structure, external financing capacity, defence expenditure, and artificial intelligence capabilities interact in shaping India’s foreign policy options and security architecture. The central argument is that national power in the contemporary international system cannot be understood through military capacity alone; it is increasingly conditioned by the composition of trade, the resilience of payment balances, the concentration of strategic dependencies, and the institutional ability to deploy data-driven statecraft. The paper therefore develops an integrated geostrategic framework that links geoeconomics, strategic autonomy, and AI-enabled governance to measurable indicators drawn from official and widely cited public datasets. The framework treats trade not simply as an economic variable but as a channel through which vulnerability, leverage, technological upgrading, and coalition-building are transmitted.
Empirically, the study draws on Indian government merchandise trade series, Reserve Bank of India balance-of-payments data, World Bank macroeconomic indicators, SIPRI defence and arms-transfer data, and Oxford Insights and industry-based evidence on AI readiness. The results show that India’s trade expansion has significantly increased the scale of national power, but that the widening merchandise deficit and the persistence of concentrated strategic import channels continue to produce vulnerabilities that require active management. At the same time, net invisibles and investment inflows have repeatedly cushioned the merchandise deficit, while defence expenditure has risen alongside a gradual reduction in arms-source concentration from Russia. On this basis, the paper proposes an innovative policy architecture in which trade diplomacy, industrial policy, maritime security, technology coalitions, and AI-assisted strategic decision systems are treated as mutually reinforcing pillars of state capacity. The analytical conclusion is that India’s external strategy will be strongest when commercial diversification, financial resilience, defence indigenisation, and sovereign AI capabilities are pursued as one integrated national power project.
Keywords: India; geostrategy; foreign policy; national power; trade security nexus; strategic autonomy; artificial intelligence; defence economics; balance of payments; supply-chain resilience

results.title: Rethinking AI in the age of climate collapse: Ethics, power, and responsibility
results.summary: The climate crisis requires responses that integrate scientific, ethical, social, and technological perspectives. Artificial intelligence (AI) has emerged as a powerful tool in climate modelling, environmental monitoring, and energy optimisation, yet its growing use also raises critical environmental, ethical, legal, and social questions. This contribution examines the ambivalent role of AI in the ecological crisis, addressing both its promises and its risks. On the one hand, AI supports improvements in climate forecasting, renewable energy management, and real-time detection of environmental degradation. On the other hand, the energy demands of data centres, resource-intensive hardware production, algorithmic bias, corporate concentration of power, and technocratic decision-making reveal contradictions that challenge its sustainability. The discussion explores these issues through interdisciplinary lenses, including environmental ethics, philosophy of technology, and legal governance, and concludes with recommendations for socially just, ecologically responsible, and democratically accountable uses of AI. Rather than assuming AI as an inherently sustainable solution, this analysis argues that its contribution to climate action depends fundamentally on the values, institutions, and power structures that shape its development.

results.title: Redistributing Decision-Making Power and Influence in the AI Ecosystem: A Call for Inclusive Participation of Users and User Organisations
results.summary:
Contemporary debates on artificial intelligence (AI) risk management and governance have been dominated by a narrow set of actors—namely large AI foundation model developers, government agencies, select non-governmental organisations, and academic institutions. While these actors shape the regulatory, ethical, and technical contours of the AI ecosystem, users and user organisations—corporations, non-profits, public institutions, and individual practitioners who deploy AI systems in real-world contexts—remain largely excluded from formal decision-making processes. This exclusion persists despite the fact that users generate the economic value, data, and operational contexts upon which AI systems depend.
This paper argues that the marginalisation of users from AI governance constitutes a structural imbalance of power with significant legal, ethical, and societal implications. Drawing on developments in AI regulation, fiduciary law, data rights, and risk management practices, the paper demonstrates how existing governance frameworks insufficiently address issues of consent, accountability, data appropriation, concentration of power, and downstream harms experienced by users. It further highlights the growing misalignment between the expanding societal deployment of AI systems and the limited participation of affected stakeholders in defining acceptable risk thresholds and governance norms.
The paper advances a normative and institutional case for inclusive AI governance, calling for the formal incorporation of users and user organisations into AI risk management, policy formulation, ethical standard-setting, and oversight mechanisms. It outlines practical pathways for achieving such inclusion, including strengthened legal rights, participatory governance processes, fiduciary accountability, external auditing, and user-driven internal AI governance frameworks. The paper concludes that redistributing decision-making power within the AI ecosystem is not merely a matter of fairness, but a structural necessity to ensure legitimacy, trust, and long-term sustainability in AI governance.
